summary: A new analysis led by Xavier Waintal at CEA Grenoble, published in Physical Review B (DOI: 10.1103/hpt6-9tnk), challenges the idea that quantum chemistry is a "killer application" for quantum computers. The study shows that the widely discussed variational quantum eigensolver algorithm requires quantum computers with nearly fault-tolerant performance to compete with classical methods. For future, fault-tolerant machines using quantum phase estimation, another issue—the "orthogonality catastrophe"—makes simulating larger molecules exponentially harder, limiting practical use. Thibaud Louvet of Quobly and George Booth of King’s College London highlight that, contrary to widespread hype, quantum chemistry will not quickly surpass classical computing, though niche uses remain possible. This finding tempers expectations for quantum computing's impact in chemistry this year.
